explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF is used instead of ON, causing row-count chatter to be sent over the network.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF allows some run-time errors to leave a transaction partially applied.
SELECT * retrieves all columns from Sales.Orders, coupling callers to schema changes and increasing bandwidth usage.
SELECT TOP (10) returns an arbitrary set of rows because no ORDER BY clause is specified to define the order.
DISTINCT is used on OrderID which is a primary key and already guaranteed unique.
WITH (NOLOCK) can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows from Sales.Orders.
YEAR() applied to the indexed OrderDate column prevents a simple range seek on the index.
Comparing int CustomerID with nvarchar @CustomerIdText forces implicit conversion and can cause performance issues.
@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID makes cardinality estimation difficult and prevents seeking.
LIKE pattern starting with % prevents a normal index seek on the Comments column.
NOT IN subquery can produce NULL results which returns all rows unexpectedly if not handled carefully.
ISNULL() wrapping CustomerID in a predicate makes the query non-sargable and prevents index usage.
Arithmetic operation on OrderID prevents direct index matching for the predicate.
Inequality <> often scans broad portions of the table instead of using an equality seek.
CROSS JOIN multiplies Orders by Customers without a join predicate, causing performance degradation.
Comma-separated table list in FROM clause obscures join predicates and makes accidental cartesian joins easier.
Scalar subquery executes a lookup for every qualifying order row instead of using a set-based join.
Correlated EXISTS check runs per outer row instead of expressing a set join which could be optimized better.
The same CASE expression is repeated in the select list and ordering logic unnecessarily.
The same CASE expression is repeated in the ORDER BY clause unnecessarily.
ORDER BY 2 refers to a column position which becomes fragile when the select list changes.
OFFSET pagination has no stable tie breaker in ORDER BY, potentially returning different rows on re-execution.
UNION performs needless duplicate removal between disjoint literal branches (OrderID = 1 vs OrderID = 2).
COUNT(*) > 0 counts all matching rows when only existence is needed; EXISTS would be more efficient.
SELECT assignment silently accepts multiple rows and keeps the last value instead of using TOP or scalar subquery.
A table variable may receive poor cardinality estimates on older compatibility levels for non-trivial data.
SELECT ... INTO creates a temp-table schema implicitly instead of declaring it explicitly with CREATE TABLE.
Uses a cursor for work that can be expressed set-wise, causing row-by-row processing overhead.
WHILE loop performs iterative work instead of a set-based operation which is more efficient in SQL Server.
Concatenates a value into executable SQL text instead of using parameterized queries.
EXEC (@Sql) executes dynamic SQL without binding parameters, exposing the code to SQL injection risks.
Concatenates a value into executable SQL text instead of using parameterized queries.
sp_executesql called with only the SQL string without defining parameters for safe execution.
Explicitly created temporary table #Scratch is not dropped before the procedure ends.
CATCH block captures an error but neither rethrows nor returns a failure status to the caller.
Uses RAISERROR rather than THROW for new error paths which is deprecated in newer SQL Server versions.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics or logging to application logs.
Embeds a business identifier (CustomerID = 1) instead of accepting it as input or looking it up.
Embeds an unlabelled date constant in query logic which is hard to maintain and understand.
Relies on session-sensitive date string conversion without an explicit style parameter or format.
Applies COALESCE in a join predicate which inhibits normal join optimization and index usage.
Self-joins Orders solely to retrieve the same key information already available in the same row.
Sorts every order by an unselective text column (Comments) which is expensive for large datasets.
Aggregates the entire fact-like order-line table without a restrictive filter causing high I/O.
Applies a row-level predicate in HAVING instead of WHERE which prevents early filtering before aggregation.
Orders a one-row aggregate result unnecessarily as ORDER BY has no effect on single row output.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; separate IF/UPDATE/INSERT blocks are preferred.
Issues a write that changes no data (Comments = Comments) and still causes logging and locking overhead.
Deletes based on a broad business value (Quantity = -1) rather than a stable key which risks data loss.
Intentionally blocks a worker instead of using asynchronous scheduling or queue-based processing.
Starts a transaction outside an error-handling boundary (TRY/CATCH) risking partial commits on failure.
Changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling which can lead to data integrity issues.
Invokes a system routine (sp_who) without schema qualification which degrades performance and is reserved.
Changes a session setting (ROWCOUNT) and does not restore it to the original state before procedure end.
LIKE pattern starting with % prevents a normal index seek on CustomerName column.
Building SQL strings by concatenating variables (@CustomerName) into executable text.
sp_executesql used without parameterized inputs for the concatenated string.
Embeds an unlabelled date constant in query logic which is hard to maintain and understand.
A scalar user-defined function called in a SELECT list forces row-by-row evaluation instead of set-based processing.
CATCH block inside a transaction does not roll back before handling the error, potentially committing partial state.
A scalar user-defined function called in a SELECT list forces row-by-row evaluation instead of set-based processing.
Explicit table/index hints (WITH INDEX) in production code can override optimizer decisions and cause performance issues.
Excessively nested subqueries (3+ levels) should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables for readability and performance.
SELECT * retrieves all columns from the table-valued function result, coupling callers to schema changes.
